reason: Verified non-refutable. The change is threshold-only (thresholds.yaml usability fp_max 0.10 -> 1.00, recall_min held at 0.90); fixture_edits=[] and prompt_rewrite='none', so no under-flagging, no loosened blocking severity, and no fixture mutation is introduced. usability IS an advisory agent: registry.py:294 sets block_threshold=None, and rubric.final.md (lines 22-24) plus evals.py:30-38 define that for block_threshold=None agents ANY finding counts only as 'surfaced' and 'an info finding on clean code is not a false positive.' The baseline 'FAIL' (fp 0.33 > 0.10) is purely a metric mismatch: scoring an engine-non-blocking advisory agent against a blocking fp_max. The three good-fixture findings driving fp 0.33 (scorecard.md:350-353, delete-with-confirm r1) are ALL severity 'info' (no success toast, window.confirm styling, non-dismissable inline error) -- by-design advisory surfacings on clean code, none of which block in production (checks.py:32 short-circuits when block_threshold is None). The new fp_max 1.00 exactly mirrors dependency and documentation (thresholds.yaml:45,48), the other two block_threshold=None advisory agents, making the metric consistent with the engine semantics. Recall is unaffected and remains protected: usability caught delete-no-confirm at r0/r1/r2 (recall 1.00 observed, scorecard.md:195-197), so no known-bad fixture slips through. No good fixture is made dirty and no bad fixture is made ambiguous because no fixture file changes. The only residual risk -- that fp_max=1.00 stops catching a future genuine over-flag -- is the accepted, documented advisory posture already in force for dependency/documentation, not a regression introduced here.
